original_abstract: While the oxidative addition of Ni(I) to aryl iodides has been commonly proposed in catalytic methods, an in-depth mechanistic understanding of this fundamental process is still lacking. Herein, we describe a detailed mechanistic study of the oxidative addition process using electroanalytical and statistical modeling techniques. Electroanalytical techniques allowed rapid measurement of the oxidative addition rates for a diverse set of aryl iodide substrates and four classes of catalytically relevant complexes (Ni(<sup>Me</sup>BPy), Ni(<sup>Me</sup>Phen), Ni(Terpy), and Ni(BPP)). With >200 experimental rate measurements, we were able to identify essential electronic and steric factors impacting the rate of oxidative addition through multivariate linear regression models. This has led to a classification of oxidative addition mechanisms, either through a three-center concerted or halogen-atom abstraction pathway based on the ligand type. A global heat map of predicted oxidative addition rates was created and shown applicable to a better understanding of the reaction outcome in a case study of a Ni-catalyzed coupling reaction.
humanized_abstract: The oxidative addition of Ni(I) to aryl iodides is widely employed in catalytic reactions; however, little is known about the mechanism. In this work, we investigated the oxidative addition process at a detailed level using a combination of electroanalytical and statistical methods. We were able to measure catalytic activity at a rate that is orders of magnitude faster than the existing methods, and we collected the largest data set of oxidative addition rates for a broad range of aryl iodides and four common ligand sets: Ni(<sup>Me</sup>BPy), Ni(<sup>Me</sup>Phen), Ni(Terpy), and Ni(BPP). With more than 200 rates collected using multiple sources and methods, we use multivariate linear regression to correlate the rates to the pertinent electronic and steric effects. The models led to a mechanism description of oxidative addition into three different paths that depend on the ligand set of the catalyst (halogen-atom abstraction or three-center concerted), and it allows us to construct a global heat map that was applicable to predict the reaction outcome in the Ni-based cross-coupling reactions, offering valuable insights on catalyst design.

With more than 200 rates collected using multiple sources and methods, we use multivariate linear regression to correlate the rates to the pertinent electronic and steric effects. The models led to a mechanism description of oxidative addition into three different paths that depend on the ligand set of the catalyst (halogen-atom abstraction or three-center concerted), and it allows us to construct a global heat map that was applicable to predict the reaction outcome in the Ni-based cross-coupling reactions, offering valuable insights on catalyst design.
